definition: To speak very softly, using breath rather than voice
examples: She leaned in to whisper the answer so no one else could hear.
The children whispered secrets behind the classroom door.
origin: From Old English hwisprian, imitating the sound itself.

definition: To look with eyes partly closed, as in bright light
examples: He squinted at the sign, trying to read it from across the street.
The sun was so strong she had to squint to see the ball.
origin: From Dutch schuinte, meaning “a slope or slant.”

definition: Truly what it is said to be; sincere and authentic
examples: Her smile was warm and genuine.
The jeweler confirmed the diamond was genuine.
origin: From Latin genuinus, meaning “innate” or “native.”

definition: A hard, shiny material such as iron, gold, or copper
examples: The bridge is built from thousands of tons of metal.
Gold is a precious metal used in jewelry.
origin: From Greek metallon, meaning “mine” or “quarry.”

definition: An untidy or confused mixture of things
examples: His desk was a jumble of papers, cables, and coffee cups.
The moving boxes held a jumble of kitchen tools and old toys.
origin: Likely imitative, first recorded in English in the 1520s.

definition: To take something with the intention of returning it
examples: Can I borrow your pen for a moment?
She borrowed money from the bank to start her shop.
origin: From Old English borgian, meaning “to lend or pledge.”

definition: Full of keen desire or enthusiasm for something
examples: The puppy was eager to play the moment we walked in.
New employees are often eager to prove themselves.
origin: From Old French aigre, meaning “sharp” or “keen.”

definition: Showing courage in the face of danger or difficulty
examples: The brave firefighter carried the child to safety.
It was brave of her to speak up in the meeting.
origin: From Italian bravo, meaning “bold” or “wild.”

definition: To express warm approval or admiration of someone
examples: The coach praised the team for their effort.
Critics praised the film's honest storytelling.
origin: From Old French preisier, meaning “to value or prize.”

definition: Feeling very tired, especially after long effort
examples: The hikers were weary after the twelve-hour climb.
She gave a weary sigh and closed her laptop.
origin: From Old English wērig, meaning “tired.”

definition: Arranged neatly and in order
examples: He keeps a tidy desk with everything in its place.
The garden looked tidy after an afternoon of weeding.
origin: From Middle English tidy, meaning “timely” or “in season.”

definition: To come together or bring things together in one place
examples: The family gathers for dinner every Sunday.
She gathered her notes before the presentation.
origin: From Old English gaderian, meaning “to bring together.”

definition: To mix things together so they combine smoothly
examples: Blend the butter and sugar until creamy.
The new building blends into the old neighborhood.
origin: From Old Norse blanda, meaning “to mix.”

definition: To take a brief or hurried look
examples: He glanced at his watch during the meeting.
She glanced over her shoulder before crossing.
origin: From Old French glacier, meaning “to slip or slide.”

definition: Firmly fixed; not shaking or changing
examples: Keep the ladder steady while I climb.
She made steady progress on the project all month.
origin: From Old English stede, meaning “place” or “position.”

definition: Producing strong, clear images in the mind; very bright
examples: She gave a vivid description of the carnival.
The painting uses vivid reds and yellows.
origin: From Latin vividus, meaning “full of life.”

definition: Not proud; modest about one's own importance
examples: Despite her fame, she stayed humble and approachable.
He gave a humble reply, crediting the whole team.
origin: From Latin humilis, meaning “low” or “from the earth.”

definition: To be carried slowly along, as by water or air
examples: The boat drifted gently down the river.
His mind drifted during the long lecture.
origin: From Old Norse drift, meaning “snowdrift” or “driving.”

definition: Giving a feeling of comfort, warmth, and relaxation
examples: We spent the evening in a cozy cabin by the fire.
The café has a cozy corner perfect for reading.
origin: From Scots colsie, of uncertain origin, 1700s.

definition: Free from worry, anger, or excitement; peaceful
examples: She stayed calm even when the alarm went off.
The sea was calm after the storm passed.
origin: From Greek kauma, “heat of the day,” when winds rest.

definition: The way a person behaves or carries themselves
examples: Her calm deportment impressed the interview panel.
The school once gave lessons in posture and deportment.
origin: From French déporter, meaning “to conduct oneself.”

definition: To sharpen; to stimulate or make keener
examples: The trailer whetted my appetite for the full film.
He whetted the knife on a stone before carving.
origin: From Old English hwettan, meaning “to sharpen.”

definition: To waste something, especially money or time, carelessly
examples: He squandered his savings on gadgets he never used.
Don't squander this chance — it won't come again.
origin: Origin unknown; first used in English in the 1580s.

definition: So perfect that no fault can be found
examples: The waiter's timing was impeccable all evening.
She arrived in an impeccable navy suit.
origin: From Latin impeccabilis, “not able to sin.”

definition: Spreading widely through every part of something
examples: The smell of fresh bread was pervasive in the bakery.
Smartphones have a pervasive influence on daily life.
origin: From Latin pervadere, meaning “to go through.”

definition: Gloomy, sullen, and unwilling to talk or smile
examples: He grew morose after the team's defeat.
The morose clerk barely looked up from the counter.
origin: From Latin morosus, meaning “peevish” or “fastidious.”

definition: Truthful and straightforward, even when it is difficult
examples: She gave a candid assessment of the project's problems.
His candid admission surprised the whole room.
origin: From Latin candidus, meaning “white” or “pure.”

definition: Able to recover quickly from difficulty or change
examples: Children are often more resilient than adults expect.
The resilient economy bounced back within a year.
origin: From Latin resilire, meaning “to leap back.”

definition: Showing extreme care about small details
examples: The accountant kept meticulous records of every expense.
Restoring the painting required meticulous work.
origin: From Latin meticulosus, meaning “fearful,” later “careful.”

definition: Open to more than one interpretation; unclear
examples: The contract's ambiguous wording led to a dispute.
Her ambiguous reply left us unsure of her plans.
origin: From Latin ambigere, meaning “to wander around.”

definition: A sentimental longing for the past
examples: The old song filled her with nostalgia for summer camp.
He collects vintage radios out of pure nostalgia.
origin: From Greek nostos “homecoming” and algos “pain.”

definition: Dealing with things practically rather than idealistically
examples: She took a pragmatic approach and fixed what she could.
The board wants pragmatic solutions, not grand visions.
origin: From Greek pragma, meaning “deed” or “act.”

definition: Fluent and persuasive in speaking or writing
examples: Her eloquent speech moved the audience to tears.
He made an eloquent case for the new library.
origin: From Latin eloqui, meaning “to speak out.”

definition: Holding firmly to something; persistent and determined
examples: The tenacious reporter followed the story for years.
Her tenacious grip on the rope saved them both.
origin: From Latin tenax, meaning “holding fast.”

definition: Careful and sparing with money or resources
examples: Years of frugal living let them buy the house outright.
He packed a frugal lunch of bread and cheese.
origin: From Latin frugalis, related to frux, “fruit” or “value.”

definition: Producing a great deal of profit
examples: Consulting proved far more lucrative than teaching.
They signed a lucrative deal with the streaming service.
origin: From Latin lucrum, meaning “gain” or “profit.”

definition: Showing steady, earnest care and effort in work
examples: The diligent student reviewed her notes every evening.
Months of diligent testing caught the flaw early.
origin: From Latin diligere, meaning “to value highly.”

definition: So delicate or precise as to be hard to notice
examples: There's a subtle difference between the two shades.
She gave him a subtle nod to begin.
origin: From Latin subtilis, meaning “finely woven.”

definition: Very skilled or proficient at something
examples: She's adept at calming nervous clients.
He became adept with the new software in days.
origin: From Latin adeptus, “one who has attained.”

definition: The ability to understand and share another's feelings
examples: Good nurses combine skill with genuine empathy.
He listened with empathy rather than judgment.
origin: From Greek empatheia, “feeling into.”

definition: A lover of words
examples: As a true logophile, she read dictionaries for fun.
The crossword club is a haven for logophiles.
origin: From Greek logos “word” and philos “loving.”

definition: Laborious study or writing, especially done late at night
examples: The thesis was the fruit of years of lucubration.
His notebooks filled with midnight lucubrations.
origin: From Latin lucubrare, “to work by lamplight.”

definition: Idealistic and impractical in a romantic, unrealistic way
examples: His quixotic plan to sail the Atlantic alone worried us.
It was a quixotic campaign, noble but doomed.
origin: From Don Quixote, Cervantes's dream-chasing knight.

definition: Having a strong spiritual or mysterious quality
examples: The cathedral's silence felt almost numinous.
Dawn over the mountains had a numinous beauty.
origin: From Latin numen, meaning “divine will” or “nod.”

definition: Lying or resting on top of something else
examples: The tunnel collapsed under the superincumbent rock.
Divers feel the superincumbent weight of the sea above.
origin: From Latin super “above” and incumbere “to lie upon.”

definition: Having beautifully shaped buttocks
examples: The statue is famed for its callipygian grace.
Critics praised the sculpture's callipygian form.
origin: From Greek kallos “beauty” and pygē “buttocks.”

definition: Lasting for a very short time
examples: The beauty of cherry blossoms is ephemeral, gone within a week.
Fame on social media tends to be ephemeral.
origin: From Greek ephēmeros, meaning “lasting only a day.”

definition: The occurrence of happy events by chance
examples: Meeting her co-founder on that delayed train was pure serendipity.
Many discoveries owe as much to serendipity as to planning.
origin: Coined by Horace Walpole from the tale “The Three Princes of Serendip.”

definition: To deliberately make something unclear or confusing
examples: The report seemed designed to obfuscate rather than explain.
Jargon can obfuscate a simple idea.
origin: From Latin obfuscare, meaning “to darken.”

definition: Having sharp insight and keen understanding
examples: A perspicacious investor saw the crash coming.
Her perspicacious questions cut to the heart of the issue.
origin: From Latin perspicax, meaning “sharp-sighted.”

definition: Habitually saying very little; reserved in speech
examples: The taciturn farmer answered in single words.
He grew taciturn whenever the topic turned personal.
origin: From Latin tacitus, meaning “silent.”

definition: Generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival
examples: She was magnanimous in victory, praising her opponent.
A magnanimous gesture ended the long feud.
origin: From Latin magnus “great” and animus “soul.”

definition: Spreading harm gradually and unnoticed
examples: Rust is insidious, eating metal from the inside.
The disease has an insidious onset with few symptoms.
origin: From Latin insidiae, meaning “ambush.”

definition: A person who flatters the powerful for personal gain
examples: The director surrounded himself with sycophants.
A sycophant praises every idea the boss utters.
origin: From Greek sykophantēs, literally “fig-shower.”

definition: Calmness and composure, especially under stress
examples: She faced the diagnosis with remarkable equanimity.
Meditation helped him keep his equanimity at work.
origin: From Latin aequus “even” and animus “mind.”

definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words
examples: The view from the summit filled them with ineffable joy.
There was an ineffable sadness in the old photographs.
origin: From Latin ineffabilis, “that cannot be spoken.”

definition: Talking a lot, especially about things that are not important
examples: The garrulous cab driver narrated the whole city tour.
He grew garrulous after his second coffee.
origin: From Latin garrire, meaning “to chatter.”

definition: The lowest point or worst moment of something
examples: Losing both sponsors marked the nadir of the team's season.
Relations between the two countries reached their nadir.
origin: From Arabic naẓīr, “opposite” — opposite the zenith.

definition: Extremely poor, or extremely unwilling to spend money
examples: The artist spent penurious years before his first sale.
Their penurious uncle reused every envelope twice.
origin: From Latin penuria, meaning “want” or “scarcity.”

definition: Widely told but probably not true
examples: The story about the CEO's garage start is likely apocryphal.
An apocryphal quote circulates with the wrong author.
origin: From Greek apokryphos, meaning “hidden away.”
